items.title: Generate a typed, hierarchical chart of accounts
items.description: The chart of accounts must be a tree of Account rows, each carrying a RootType (Asset, Liability, Equity, Income, Expense) and an IsGroup flag. Only non-group (leaf) accounts may receive postings; group accounts exist solely to roll up balances. Account number plus company scope is the natural key.
items.applicability: The foundational entity the generator emits before any posting document; every voucher line references a leaf account.
items.example: Assets (group) > Current Assets (group) > Cash and Bank (group) > HDFC Current A/c (leaf, postable). A journal line targeting 'Current Assets' is rejected because it is a group.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce IsGroup-cannot-post and parent-RootType-consistency in the domain layer, not only the UI. Test implication: assert posting to a group account throws and that a child cannot have a RootType different from its parent. Does not prescribe a specific national chart; only the structural invariants.

items.title: Derive normal balance side from root type
items.description: Asset and Expense accounts normally carry debit balances; Liability, Equity, and Income accounts normally carry credit balances. The generator should compute a signed display balance and may flag accounts that drift to an abnormal side as a soft warning, never a hard block.
items.applicability: Account balance computation, ledger reports, and dashboard tiles in a generated accounting module.
items.example: A Bank account (Asset) showing a credit balance is rendered with a warning badge, indicating a likely overdraft or misposting, but the figure is still shown truthfully.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store amounts as signed-by-side or as separate debit/credit columns consistently; do not mix conventions. Test implication: verify normal-side derivation for each root type. Abnormal balances are legitimate in some cases (overdrafts, contra accounts), so this must remain advisory.

items.title: Journal entry is the canonical balanced voucher
items.description: Model a Journal Entry as a header plus two or more lines, each with an account, a debit amount and a credit amount (one of which is zero per line). Save validation requires sum(debit) == sum(credit) within currency tolerance, at least two lines, and at least one debit and one credit.
items.applicability: The base posting document; invoices, payments and stock vouchers are specialised journal entries that reduce to GL lines.
items.example: Manual JE: Debit Rent Expense 5000; Credit Bank 5000. Submitting writes two GL entries with the same voucher reference and posting date.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: implement the balance check in a single domain method reused by every posting document so the rule cannot be bypassed. Test implication: parametrised tests for unbalanced, single-line, and zero-total entries all rejecting. Does not enforce correct account selection, only structural balance.

items.title: Post through a single GL posting engine
items.description: All documents that affect the ledger must route through one posting service that writes immutable GLEntry rows (account, debit, credit, posting date, voucher type, voucher number, cost centre, company). No controller or repository writes GL rows directly.
items.applicability: Submission path of every accounting document in a generated ERP.
items.example: SalesInvoice.Submit() builds a list of GL lines and calls GeneralLedger.Post(voucher); the same method is called by Payment, JournalEntry and StockEntry submission.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: wrap the GL write and the document status change in one database transaction so a partial post cannot occur. Test implication: simulate a mid-post failure and assert no orphan GL rows remain. The engine does not decide account mapping; callers supply lines.

items.title: Store money as fixed-precision decimal
items.description: All monetary columns must be decimal with explicit precision and scale (e.g. decimal(18,2) for amounts, decimal(18,6) for rates and exchange rates). Never use float or double for money. Round only at defined boundaries using a documented rounding mode.
items.applicability: Every amount, rate, and balance column the generator emits for an accounting module.
items.example: EF Core mapping: builder.Property(x => x.Amount).HasPrecision(18, 2). A rate of 0.333333 is stored at scale 6 and the line amount rounded to scale 2 on calculation.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: configure precision via EF model configuration, not just CLR types, so the SQL column matches. Test implication: assert no float columns exist on financial tables and that repeated additions do not drift. Rounding policy choice (half-up vs banker's) is a business decision the generator must surface, not silently pick.

items.title: Trial balance is the sum of GL entries per account
items.description: Generate the trial balance by aggregating GLEntry debit and credit per account for a date range, then presenting opening, period movement, and closing per account. The grand total of debits must equal the grand total of credits; a non-zero difference signals a posting defect.
items.applicability: Core report in any generated accounting module and the integrity check for the whole ledger.
items.example: For the fiscal year, the report lists each leaf account with its closing debit or credit; the footer asserts total debit == total credit and shows the difference as zero.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute with per-account aggregation queries, not a single group-by-constant; in this codebase prefer separate accumulations to avoid known SQL Server hangs. Test implication: seed balanced vouchers and assert the trial-balance difference is exactly zero. Does not detect correctly-balanced but mis-classified postings.

items.title: Classify accounts into P&L versus Balance Sheet by root type
items.description: Income and Expense accounts flow to the Profit & Loss statement and are reset each fiscal year; Asset, Liability, and Equity accounts flow to the Balance Sheet and carry balances forward across years. The classification is driven entirely by RootType.
items.applicability: Report generation and the year-end close routine in a generated accounting module.
items.example: P&L = sum(Income) - sum(Expense) for the period. Net profit is posted to a Retained Earnings (Equity) account so the Balance Sheet balances after close.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: tag each account's statement at the entity level so reports and close logic share one source of truth. Test implication: assert P&L accounts have zero opening balance after a year-end close and Balance Sheet accounts carry forward. Statement layout standards vary by jurisdiction and are out of scope.

items.title: Balance sheet must balance after close
items.description: Assets must equal Liabilities plus Equity (including current-period retained earnings) at any reporting date. The generator should expose this identity as a computed check in the balance-sheet report and fail loudly if it does not hold.
items.applicability: Balance-sheet report and post-close validation in a generated accounting module.
items.example: Report footer computes Assets - (Liabilities + Equity); the value must be zero. If non-zero, an unbalanced voucher or an unposted period bridge entry exists.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include current-year profit in the equity side before comparing, otherwise the check falsely fails mid-year. Test implication: end-to-end test posting a full cycle then asserting the identity holds. Does not validate the correctness of individual classifications.

items.title: Route customer balances through an AR control account
items.description: Sales invoices debit a single Accounts Receivable control account while the per-customer outstanding is tracked in a subledger keyed by customer and voucher. The control-account GL balance must always equal the sum of open customer balances.
items.applicability: Sales invoicing and customer-statement generation in a generated ERP.
items.example: Invoice to ACME debits AR-control 1180 and records an AR subledger row for ACME; the customer ledger and the GL control account reconcile to the same total.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never let users post directly to the AR control account via manual journal entries unless mirrored in the subledger. Test implication: a reconciliation test asserting control-account balance equals subledger sum after a batch of invoices and receipts. Multi-currency receivables require per-currency control accounts or revaluation, out of scope here.

items.title: Route supplier balances through an AP control account
items.description: Purchase invoices credit a single Accounts Payable control account while per-supplier outstanding is tracked in a subledger. The control-account GL balance must equal the sum of open supplier balances at all times.
items.applicability: Purchase invoicing and supplier-statement generation in a generated ERP.
items.example: Bill from a vendor credits AP-control and records an AP subledger row; paying it debits AP-control and credits Bank, clearing both the subledger row and the control balance by the same amount.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: mirror every AP control posting in the subledger so reconciliation cannot drift. Test implication: assert AP-control equals supplier-ledger sum after invoice-then-payment cycles. Withholding-tax and partial-payment apportionment are policy details the generator must parametrise, not hardcode.

items.title: Model payment entry with allocation against outstanding documents
items.description: A Payment Entry moves money between a party account and a bank or cash account and carries allocation lines linking the paid amount to specific outstanding invoices. The sum of allocations plus any unallocated advance must equal the payment amount.
items.applicability: Customer receipts and supplier payments in a generated ERP accounting module.
items.example: Receive 1000 from ACME: allocate 700 to INV-1 and 300 to INV-2; GL posts Debit Bank 1000, Credit AR-control 1000, and both invoices' outstanding reduce accordingly.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate that no allocation exceeds an invoice's remaining outstanding and recompute outstanding atomically under the same transaction. Test implication: over-allocation and double-allocation must be rejected. Advance handling and write-offs are explicit features the generator must add deliberately, not assume.

items.title: Generate a payment-reconciliation skeleton, not a guess
items.description: Bank reconciliation should match imported bank lines to GL bank-account entries by amount, date window, and reference, proposing matches for human confirmation. The generator emits the matching skeleton and a manual-match fallback; it must not auto-clear unmatched lines.
items.applicability: Bank and cash reconciliation in a generated ERP.
items.example: A bank statement line of 1000 on 2026-06-20 proposes a match to a Payment Entry of 1000 dated 2026-06-19 with reference 'ACME'; the user confirms to set the entry's clearance date.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store a clearance date on the GL bank entry rather than mutating the posted amount. Test implication: assert proposed matches require confirmation and that unmatched lines stay open. Fuzzy auto-matching heuristics are explicitly out of scope and would need separate validation before trust.

items.title: Attach a cost-centre dimension to expense and income postings
items.description: GL entries on Income and Expense accounts should carry an optional cost-centre (and optionally project) dimension so profitability can be reported by segment without changing the chart of accounts. Cost centres are a hierarchy parallel to accounts.
items.applicability: Posting documents and segment-profitability reports in a generated ERP.
items.example: A travel expense of 300 is posted to Travel Expense with cost centre 'Sales-North', enabling a P&L filtered to that cost centre.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make the dimension nullable and indexed; do not force a cost centre on balance-sheet postings where it adds no meaning. Test implication: assert a cost-centre-filtered P&L sums only matching entries. Allocation of shared overhead across cost centres is a separate feature, not implied here.

items.title: Define fiscal years and lockable accounting periods
items.description: The generator must emit a FiscalYear entity and period locks. Posting is allowed only into an open period; a closed or locked period rejects new vouchers and edits. Posting date, not creation date, determines the period.
items.applicability: All posting documents and the period-close routine in a generated ERP.
items.example: After month-end, June is locked; an attempt to post a journal entry dated 2026-06-15 is rejected with a clear 'period closed' domain error.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: check the period lock inside the posting engine so every document type is covered uniformly. Test implication: assert posting into a locked period fails for each document type. Back-dated adjustment workflows with elevated permission are a deliberate feature to add, not an exception to assume.

items.title: Year-end close transfers P&L to retained earnings
items.description: Closing a fiscal year posts a closing voucher that zeroes Income and Expense accounts and transfers the net result to a Retained Earnings equity account, so the next year's P&L opens at zero while balance-sheet accounts carry forward.
items.applicability: Year-end close routine in a generated accounting module.
items.example: Net profit 40000 posts: Debit each Income account to zero, Credit each Expense account to zero, net Credit Retained Earnings 40000.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make the close idempotent and reversible (a cancellable closing voucher) so a premature close can be undone. Test implication: assert P&L accounts open the next year at zero and equity increases by the net result. Distribution of profit (dividends) is a separate posting, not part of close.

items.title: Seed opening balances through a balanced opening voucher
items.description: Migrating balances into a new ERP must be done with a single balanced opening journal entry per company, using a Temporary Opening (equity) account as the balancing line, rather than editing account balances directly. Subledger opening balances feed the corresponding control accounts.
items.applicability: Initial data load and go-live for a generated accounting module.
items.example: Opening: Debit Bank 50000, Debit AR-control 12000, Credit AP-control 8000, Credit Temporary Opening 54000; the entry balances and is dated the day before go-live.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: the Temporary Opening account should net to zero once all opening entries are posted, signalling a complete migration. Test implication: assert the opening voucher balances and that the temporary account zeroes after subledger openings. Does not validate that the migrated figures themselves are correct.

items.title: Model bank and cash as dedicated ledger accounts
items.description: Each physical bank account and each cash box must be its own leaf Asset account flagged as a bank/cash account, so payments, reconciliation, and cash-position reports can target them specifically. A Mode-of-Payment maps user-facing payment methods to these accounts.
items.applicability: Payment entries, point-of-sale postings, and treasury reports in a generated ERP.
items.example: Modes of payment 'Cash', 'HDFC Card', 'Bank Transfer' map to the Cash, HDFC Bank, and ICICI Bank accounts respectively; a receipt picks a mode and posts to the mapped account.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: flag bank/cash accounts on the entity so reconciliation only offers valid targets. Test implication: assert a payment can only select a bank/cash-flagged account. Cash-flow forecasting and multi-bank sweeps are separate features.

items.title: Drive invoice GL impact from a posting template
items.description: A sales invoice should expand into GL lines from a deterministic template: debit AR-control for the gross, credit each income account for the net per line, and credit each tax account for tax. The reverse template applies to purchase invoices. Templates make posting auditable and testable.
items.applicability: Sales and purchase invoice submission in a generated ERP.
items.example: Invoice net 850 + tax 150: Debit AR-control 1000, Credit Sales Revenue 850, Credit Output Tax 150. Gross equals the AR debit by construction.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute the gross as net + tax and assert it equals the control-account line so rounding cannot break the balance. Test implication: assert the generated GL lines balance and match the template for each tax scenario. Discounts, freight, and rounding adjustments must each be explicit template lines, not hidden adjustments.

items.title: Compute taxes from a configurable tax template
items.description: Taxes must come from a tax template of rows (rate, tax account, on-net or cumulative basis) applied to the taxable amount, posting each tax to its own GL account. Rates and accounts are data, never hardcoded constants in the posting logic.
items.applicability: Sales and purchase document totalling and GL posting in a generated ERP.
items.example: An 18% template on a net of 850 produces tax 153 posted to Output Tax 18%; changing the rate is a data edit, not a code change.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store rate as decimal and round tax per line to the currency scale, then reconcile to the document total. Test implication: assert tax recomputes when the template changes and that per-line rounding sums to the header tax. The pack does not assert correctness of any specific jurisdiction's tax rules or filing.

items.title: Assign immutable document numbers from a naming series
items.description: Each accounting document type draws its number from a per-company, per-fiscal-year naming series with a prefix and a zero-padded counter. Numbers are assigned at submit, are gap-free where law requires, and are never reused or reassigned.
items.applicability: Submission of invoices, payments, journal entries and any auditable voucher in a generated ERP.
items.example: Series 'SINV-26-' yields SINV-26-00001, SINV-26-00002; cancelling SINV-26-00001 leaves the number burned, not recycled.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: allocate the next number inside the submit transaction to avoid races; consider a dedicated counter row with row-level locking. Test implication: concurrent submits must not produce duplicate numbers. Whether gaps are permitted is a jurisdictional policy the generator must expose as configuration.

items.title: Scope every accounting row by company
items.description: All accounts, vouchers and GL entries carry a CompanyId, and every query and report filters by company. Cross-company postings are only permitted through an explicit inter-company journal entry that balances within each company.
items.applicability: Data model and every report in a multi-company generated ERP.
items.example: A user switched to Company A never sees Company B's trial balance; an inter-company charge posts a paired due-to/due-from entry in each company.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the company filter with a query filter or repository guard so a forgotten WHERE clause cannot leak data. Test implication: assert a report for one company excludes another's entries. Consolidated multi-company reporting is a separate, deliberate feature.

items.title: Build accounting list views from lightweight projections
items.description: Ledger and document list views must project only the columns the grid shows (number, date, party, amount, status) into a record/DTO and must never materialise large text bodies. Aggregates use separate count and sum queries rather than group-by-constant projections.
items.applicability: All list and report endpoints in a generated accounting module, especially on SQL Server.
items.example: The invoice list selects new { InvoiceNo, Date, Customer, GrandTotal, Status } and pages it; it does not load remarks, terms, or line collections.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: define a projection record per list and select into it; in this codebase avoid GroupBy(_ => 1) which has caused indefinite page hangs. Test implication: assert list endpoints return quickly and do not select large text columns. This is a performance and reliability guard, not a functional feature.
